inputs.userGoal: Provision a jumpbox VM for secure management, establish NSGs for each tier, and connect tiers using internal Azure Load Balancer
subGoals: Production-grade: zone redundancy across availability zones 1/2/3, no single point of failure
Security-first: no public IPs on workload VMs, Azure Bastion + Key Vault for SSH keys, Trusted Launch, managed identity, per-tier NSG segmentation
Operational excellence baseline: Log Analytics, NSG flow logs, boot diagnostics, NAT Gateway for deterministic egress
Classic 3-tier architecture (web/app/db) on Linux Ubuntu 22.04 VMSS with internal load balancers between tiers, fronted by Application Gateway WAF v2
insightsApplied: 96.1% (563 of 585) of resources reporting properties.minimumTlsVersion are pinned to TLS 1.2 (TLS1_2 or 1.2); only 3.9% still report TLS 1.0.

Storage accounts overwhelmingly enforce secure-transport and block anonymous blob access: supportsHttpsTrafficOnly is True on 460 of 464 accounts (99.1%) and allowBlobPublicAccess is False on 463 of 464 accounts (99.8%).
81.3% (279 of 343) of storage accounts reporting properties.allowSharedKeyAccess have it disabled (False), and 91.5% (292 of 319) have allowCrossTenantReplication disabled.
Storage redundancy SKUs lean local-only: Standard_LRS accounts for 233 of 419 (55.6%) of storage SKUs, with Standard_GRS (24.6%) and Standard_RAGRS (19.8%) making up the rest.
97.5% (389 of 399) of storage accounts reporting properties.accessTier use the Hot tier; only 2.5% use Cool.

Key Vaults universally enable soft-delete (172 of 172, 100%), 92.5% (123 of 133) keep softDeleteRetentionInDays at 90, 84.2% (16 of 19) reporting enablePurgeProtection have it True, and 60.3% (105 of 174) leave enableRbacAuthorization False (i.e., still use access policies).
Compute SKU usage is dominated by the Standard tier (763 of 1,221 sku.tier reports, 62.5%), followed by Regional (15.6%) and Basic (11.8%); Premium tiers together account for under 6%.

architecturePrinciples: Highly available: Zone-redundant across AZs 1/2/3 for compute and traffic-tier resources
Secure: No public IPs on workload VMs; Bastion-only admin access; per-tier NSG least-privilege; Trusted Launch; managed identity; private endpoint to Key Vault
Defense in depth: Application Gateway WAF v2 + per-tier NSGs + internal LB segmentation between tiers
Operationally observable: Log Analytics + NSG flow logs + boot diagnostics on every VM
Cost-aware: Standard SKU compute, Standard_LRS for diagnostic/log storage, single NAT Gateway egress
plan.validation: All resources are deployable to East US 2 in a single resource group. Subnet CIDRs do not overlap. AzureBastionSubnet is named exactly and sized /26 per the Bastion requirement. AppGw subnet is dedicated. NSGs are not associated to the AppGw subnet in a way that blocks GatewayManager:65200-65535. Standard SKU public IPs are paired with Standard SKU LBs and AppGw v2. NAT Gateway is associated to web/app/db/jump subnets for deterministic egress (NAT Gateway takes precedence over LB outbound rules). Internal LBs are zone-redundant Standard SKU with frontends in the destination tier subnet (lbi-app frontend in snet-app, lbi-db frontend in snet-db). All VMSS use Flexible orchestration mode with zones [1,2,3]. Managed identity is referenced via userAssignedIdentities on every workload VM/VMSS. Key Vault has purge protection, RBAC auth, and is private-endpoint-only.

overallReasoning.summary: Classic 3-tier topology in a single VNet (10.10.0.0/16) in East US 2 with one subnet per tier plus dedicated subnets for Application Gateway, jumpbox, and Azure Bastion. Public ingress terminates at Application Gateway WAF v2 (zone-redundant, autoscale 2-10) which routes to vmss-web. Tier-to-tier traffic flows through Standard zone-redundant internal load balancers (lbi-app between web→app, lbi-db between app→db). All workload VMs are Linux Ubuntu 22.04, VMSS Flexible orchestration distributed across zones 1/2/3, with Trusted Launch, encryption at host, accelerated networking, and a shared user-assigned managed identity for Key Vault access. Administrators reach the jumpbox via Azure Bastion (no public IP on jumpbox or workload VMs). Per-tier NSGs enforce least-privilege ingress: AppGw→web, web→app, app→db, and Bastion/jumpbox→all tiers on SSH. Egress is funneled through a NAT Gateway for deterministic outbound IPs. Key Vault is private-endpoint-only with RBAC auth and purge protection. Log Analytics receives diagnostic settings + NSG flow logs.
overallReasoning.tradeoffs: Region: user explicitly chose East US 2 over the tenant-dominant westus2 (insight-001). Key Vault auth: enabled RBAC instead of the tenant default of access policies (insight-008) — security-first sub-goal mandates the stronger posture per the planner's mandatory security rule. Key Vault network access: set to Disabled with private endpoint, overriding insight-003 (publicNetworkAccess Enabled default) — security-first override. Storage local auth: shared-key disabled on the diagnostics storage account, overriding insight-010 — security-first override. Zone redundancy: enabled across compute and traffic resources, overriding insight-011 — user explicitly requested production zone-redundant. DB tier on VMSS rather than a managed PaaS database (Azure SQL/PostgreSQL Flexible Server) per the user's 'all VMs/VMSS' requirement; managed PaaS would offer better RPO/RTO, automated backup, and patching but was out of scope. NAT Gateway is zonal (zone 1) since NAT Gateway does not support zone-redundant deployment; for stricter zone-failure tolerance, replace with Standard LB outbound rules. Single resource group simplifies blast radius management but couples lifecycle of network and compute. Application Gateway uses a public frontend per user request — for fully internal workloads, switch to private-only AppGw.

reasoning.whyChosen: Single resource group simplifies lifecycle and IAM scoping for a single-environment workload (Operational Excellence).
reasoning.tradeoffs: Couples network and compute lifecycle but reduces operational complexity for a single workload.

reasoning.whyChosen: Central sink for VM Insights, NSG flow logs, AppGw and LB diagnostic settings (Operational Excellence).
reasoning.tradeoffs: Single workspace simplifies queries but creates a single audit boundary.

reasoning.whyChosen: Shared user-assigned identity for all workload VMs and VMSS to access Key Vault and Storage without secrets (Security).
reasoning.tradeoffs: User-assigned simplifies role assignments and survives VM recreation; broader blast radius if identity is compromised.

reasoning.whyChosen: Stores SSH public keys, AppGw TLS certificates, and any secrets. RBAC + private endpoint + purge protection enforce a defense-in-depth posture (Security).
alternativesConsidered: Access policies (insight-008 default) — weaker; rejected per mandatory security rule
reasoning.tradeoffs: RBAC requires explicit role assignments per workload; chosen to satisfy security-first sub-goal.

reasoning.whyChosen: Hosts boot diagnostics for VMs and NSG flow logs. Standard_LRS, Hot, HTTPS-only matches insights 4/5/6/7 for cost and security baseline.
alternativesConsidered: Premium SSD diagnostic storage (not allowed for boot diag)
reasoning.tradeoffs: LRS is region-local; acceptable for diagnostics. Network ACLs left Allow per insight-009 for AzureMonitor/flow log writes.

reasoning.whyChosen: /16 provides headroom for tier subnets, dedicated AppGw and Bastion subnets, and a jumpbox subnet without overlap.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Single VNet simplifies routing and NSG design; loses tier-isolation at the VNet boundary.

reasoning.whyChosen: Dedicated subnet for Application Gateway v2 per pairing constraint (no other resources allowed).
reasoning.tradeoffs: /24 supports up to 125-instance autoscale headroom.

reasoning.whyChosen: Hosts vmss-web; /24 covers Flexible orchestration up to 250 instances.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Generous sizing for future scale.

reasoning.whyChosen: Hosts vmss-app, lbi-app frontend, and the Key Vault private endpoint.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Co-locating PE with app tier minimizes VNet sprawl; privateEndpointNetworkPolicies=Enabled lets NSG apply.

reasoning.whyChosen: Hosts vmss-db and lbi-db frontend; isolated from web/app at NSG layer.
alternativesConsidered: No internal LB on DB tier
reasoning.tradeoffs: Internal LB allows stateless app→db routing and graceful DB instance maintenance.

reasoning.whyChosen: Small dedicated subnet for the management jumpbox; NAT Gateway provides patching egress.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Dedicated subnet enables targeted NSG and route policy.

reasoning.whyChosen: Mandatory subnet name and /26 minimum for Azure Bastion Standard SKU. NSG attached per pairing constraint.
alternativesConsidered: Bastion Developer SKU (no subnet) — limits to single VNet, weaker SLAs
reasoning.tradeoffs: Standard SKU required for cross-VNet support and VM scale features.

reasoning.whyChosen: Mandatory NSG rules for Azure Bastion Standard SKU: HTTPS from Internet, GatewayManager, LB probes, intra-host comms (8080/5701), SSH/RDP outbound to VNet, AzureCloud 443, session info to Internet:80.
alternativesConsidered: Omit NSG (default rules sufficient) — but pairing constraint requires explicit NSG
reasoning.tradeoffs: Explicit NSG documents Bastion's required surface and supports flow logging.

reasoning.whyChosen: AppGw v2 mandatory rules: GatewayManager 65200-65535 + AzureLoadBalancer probes; only HTTPS from Internet (no HTTP redirect at NSG layer — handled by AppGw).
reasoning.tradeoffs: AppGw will handle HTTP→HTTPS redirect via listener rules.

reasoning.whyChosen: Web tier accepts intra-subnet, AppGw subnet on 443, LB probes, and SSH from jumpbox; DenyOtherVNet at 4000 blocks lateral traffic from app/db/Bastion subnets while preserving default DenyAllInBound.
alternativesConsidered: No explicit deny (relies on default Allow VNet — too permissive)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Explicit DenyOtherVNet enforces tier segmentation.

reasoning.whyChosen: App tier accepts intra-subnet, web subnet on 8080, LB probes, and SSH from jumpbox. KV PE traffic bypasses NSG (privateEndpointNetworkPolicies=Disabled on snet-app).
reasoning.tradeoffs: Tight per-tier restriction supports zero-trust posture.

reasoning.whyChosen: DB tier accepts intra-subnet (replication), app subnet on 5432 (PostgreSQL placeholder), LB probes, and SSH from jumpbox.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Engine port is a placeholder; adjust per actual DB engine.

reasoning.whyChosen: Jumpbox accepts SSH only from AzureBastionSubnet — no other source can reach it.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Bastion-only access removes attack surface from public internet.

reasoning.whyChosen: Standard Static required for NAT Gateway pairing.
alternativesConsidered: Public IP prefix for contiguous SNAT range
reasoning.tradeoffs: Single PIP is simpler; prefix improves SNAT port budget for very high outbound load.

reasoning.whyChosen: Provides deterministic outbound SNAT for web/app/db/jump subnets and avoids SNAT port exhaustion (Reliability).
alternativesConsidered: LB outbound rules (zone-redundant but more complex)
reasoning.tradeoffs: NAT Gateway is zonal — pinned to zone 1; zone failure removes egress until failover.

reasoning.whyChosen: Required for Azure Bastion Standard SKU.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Standard SKU enables connect-to-VM features and scaling.

reasoning.whyChosen: Single managed entry point for SSH/RDP to all VMs in the VNet without exposing public IPs (Security).
alternativesConsidered: Public IP on jumpbox with NSG allowlist
reasoning.tradeoffs: Bastion has steady cost but eliminates public attack surface and simplifies audit.

reasoning.whyChosen: AppGw v2 requires Standard Static; zone-redundant for HA.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Zone-redundant PIP withstands single-AZ outage.

reasoning.whyChosen: Prevention mode with OWASP 3.2 baseline blocks common web attacks (Security).
reasoning.tradeoffs: Prevention may false-positive; tune exclusions during cutover.

reasoning.whyChosen: WAF v2 zone-redundant fronts the web tier; autoscale handles burst traffic; TLS 1.2+ predefined policy enforces modern ciphers (Reliability + Security + Performance).
reasoning.tradeoffs: WAF adds latency on large uploads; Front Door would add multi-region capability not required here.

reasoning.whyChosen: Internal Standard zone-redundant LB between web and app tiers. HTTP health probe gives richer signals than TCP. Outbound SNAT disabled because NAT Gateway provides egress (Reliability).
reasoning.tradeoffs: Adds a hop but enables graceful app-tier rolling updates and zone failover.

reasoning.whyChosen: Internal Standard zone-redundant LB between app and DB tiers; longer idle timeout suits DB connections. Outbound SNAT disabled — egress via NAT Gateway.
reasoning.tradeoffs: ILB enables transparent DB failover but client connections must reconnect on backend swap.

reasoning.whyChosen: Flexible orch + zones 1/2/3 distributes web tier across AZs (Reliability). Trusted Launch + encryption at host + accelerated networking align with security and performance baselines.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Flexible mode is the modern recommendation; sacrifices some Uniform-only features (e.g., automatic OS upgrades) for zone flexibility.

reasoning.whyChosen: Same hardening as web tier; backend of internal LB lbi-app for transparent web→app routing.
reasoning.tradeoffs: VMSS chosen per user requirement; container apps would simplify scale and patching.

reasoning.whyChosen: DB tier on VMSS per user 'all VMs/VMSS' requirement; D4s_v5 with 256GB Premium SSD data disk caching=None for ACID write workloads.
alternativesConsidered: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (better RPO/RTO)
reasoning.tradeoffs: PaaS DB would be safer and lower-ops but was excluded by the user choosing all-VMs. Engine install/replication is the user's responsibility.

reasoning.whyChosen: Single NIC for jumpbox VM in snet-jump with NIC-level NSG aligned to subnet NSG.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Single NIC keeps jumpbox simple; sufficient for SSH-only role.

reasoning.whyChosen: Burstable B2s suits low-utilization admin role (Cost). Zone 1 placement matches NAT Gateway zone for predictable outbound. Trusted Launch and managed identity match workload baseline.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Single-zone VM is acceptable for ops tooling; admins can re-provision quickly via IaC if zone fails.

reasoning.whyChosen: Removes Key Vault from the public internet; only the VNet can resolve it via private DNS (Security).
reasoning.tradeoffs: PE adds DNS configuration overhead but is the strongest network isolation.

reasoning.whyChosen: Required private DNS zone for Key Vault private endpoint resolution.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Private zone auto-registers via privateDnsZoneGroups.

reasoning.whyChosen: Links vault private DNS zone to the VNet so workload VMs resolve KV's private IP.
reasoning.tradeoffs: VNet link is the simplest single-VNet pattern.
